23.91% ([[Nielsen ratings]]){{cite news|url=https://variety.com/2012/tv/news/crystal-social-media-fuel-oscar-ratings-1118050758/ |title=Crystal, social media fuel Oscar ratings |last=Kissell |first=Rick |date=February 27, 2012 |access-date=April 26, 2012 |work=Variety |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130926040411/http://variety.com/2012/tv/news/crystal-social-media-fuel-oscar-ratings-1118050758/ |archive-date=September 26, 2013}} | last = 83rd | next = 85th }} The '''84th Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), honored the best [[2011 in film|films of 2011]] in the United States and took place on February 26, 2012, at the [[Hollywood and Highland Center Theatre]]{{ref label|DolbyTheatre|a|a2}} in [[Hollywood, Los Angeles]], beginning at 5:30 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] / 8:30 p.m. [[Eastern Time Zone|EST]]. During the ceremony, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences presented [[Academy Awards]] (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 24 categories. The ceremony was televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], and produced by [[Brian Grazer]] and [[Don Mischer]], with Mischer also serving as director. Actor [[Billy Crystal]] hosted the show for the ninth time. He first presided over the [[62nd Academy Awards|62nd ceremony]] held in 1990 and had last hosted the [[76th Academy Awards|76th ceremony]] held in 2004.{{cite news|url=https://variety.com/2011/scene/awards/crystal-confirmed-as-oscars-host-1118045965/ |title=Crystal confirmed as Oscars host |last=Grosz |first=Christy |date=November 10, 2011 |access-date=April 26, 2012 |work=Variety |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111112061951/http://www.variety.com/article/VR1118045965/ |archive-date=November 12, 2011 }} On June 14, 2011, academy president [[Tom Sherak]] announced at a press conference that, in an attempt to further revitalize interest surrounding the awards, the 2012 ceremony would feature between five and ten [[Academy Award for Best Picture|Best Picture]] nominees depending on voting results, as opposed to a set number of nominees.{{cite news|first1=Nicole |last1=Sperling |first2=Amy The telecast garnered more than 39 million viewers in the United States. ==Winners and nominees== The nominees for the 84th Academy Awards were announced on January 24, 2012, at 5:38 a.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] (13:38 [[UTC]]) at the [[Samuel Goldwyn Theater]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]], by [[Tom Sherak]], president of the academy, and the actress [[Jennifer Lawrence]].{{cite news|first=Lauren|last=Schutte|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/jennifer-lawrence-hunger-games-oscars-nominations-279717|title='Hunger Games' Star Jennifer Lawrence to Announce Academy Awards Nominations|work=The Hollywood Reporter|access-date=July 27, 2012|date=January 9, 2012|archive-date=January 12, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120112031050/http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/jennifer-lawrence-hunger-games-oscars-nominations-279717|url-status=live}} ''[[Hugo (film)|Hugo]]'' led all nominees with eleven nominations; ''[[The Artist (film)|The Artist]]'' came in second with ten.{{cite news|url= https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-16699960|title= Oscars: Hugo has 11 nominations, The Artist has 10|agency= [[BBC News]]|access-date= August 6, 2012|date= January 24, 2012|archive-date= May 23, 2014|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20140523081257/http://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-16699960|url-status= live}} The winners were announced during the awards ceremony on February 26, 2012.{{cite news|first=Lisa |last=Kennedy |url=http://www.denverpost.com/ci_20052116 |title=Silent movie gets loudest praise at the Academy Awards |work=The Denver Post |access-date=February 28, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141016180412/http://www.denverpost.com/ci_20052116 |archive-date=October 16, 2014|url-status=live}} ''The Artist'' was the second silent feature to win Best Picture. The 1927 film ''[[Wings (1927 film)|Wings]]'' was the first such film to achieve this distinction at the [[1st Academy Awards|inaugural awards ceremony]] in 1929.{{cite news|last=Day |first=Patrick Kevin |title=Oscars 2012: 'The Artist' wins for best picture |url=http://latimesblogs.latimes.com/movies/2012/02/oscars-2012-best-picture.html |work=Los Angeles Times |access-date=May 9, 2013 Moreover, it was also the first black-and-white feature to win Best Picture since 1993's ''[[Schindler's List]]''.{{ref label|TheArtist|b}} Best Actor winner [[Jean Dujardin]] became the first French Southern California Children's Chorus || Performers || "[[What a Wonderful World]]" during the annual ''[[List of Latin phrases: I#in memoriam|In Memoriam]]'' tribute |} == Ceremony information == [[File:Billy Crystal by Gage Skidmore.jpg|thumb|right|180px|[[Billy Crystal]] hosted the 84th Academy Awards|alt=Photo of Billy Crystal.]] Because of the declining viewership of recent Academy Awards ceremonies, the academy sought ideas to revamp the show while renewing interest with the nominated films. In light of the [[83rd Academy Awards|previous year's telecast]], whose performance by co-hosts [[James Franco]] and [[Anne Hathaway]] yielded critically negative reviews and a 9% decline in viewership, many within the Motion Picture Academy proposed new ways to give the awards a more populist appeal.{{cite magazine |url=http://insidemovies.ew.com/2011/06/17/as-the-oscars-change-they-lose-identity/ |title=The Oscars rejiggered AGAIN? The more this show changes, the more it loses its identity |date=June 17, 2011 |first=Owen |last=Gleiberman |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |access-date=April 29, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602045641/http://insidemovies.ew.com/2011/06/17/as-the-oscars-change-they-lose-identity/ |archive-date=June 2, 2013}}{{cite news|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/zackomalleygreenburg/2011/02/27/james-franco-anne-hathaway-and-the-convergence-of-oscar-and-grammy/ |title=James Franco, Anne Hathaway, and the Convergence of Oscar and Grammy |access-date=April 29, 2013 |last=Greenburg |first=Zack O'Malley |work=[[Forbes]] |date=February 27, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130620090102/http://www.forbes.com/sites/zackomalleygreenburg/2011/02/27/james-franco-anne-hathaway-and-the-convergence-of-oscar-and-grammy/ |archive-date=June 20, 2013 }} After a two-year experiment with ten Best Pictures nominees, AMPAS president [[Tom Sherak]] announced that the number of final nominees can now range from five to ten as opposed a fixed number. The nomination voting process would be the same as before, through [[Instant-runoff voting|preferential balloting]], but now only films that receive a minimum of 5% of total number-one votes are eligible for Best Picture nominations.{{cite magazine|url=http://insidemovies.ew.com/2012/01/24/oscars-best-picture-why-nine-nominees/ |title=Best Picture Oscar: So why are there nine nominees? |date=January 24, 2012 |first=John |last=Young |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |access-date=April 19, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130503193256/http://insidemovies.ew.com/2012/01/24/oscars-best-picture-why-nine-nominees/ |archive-date=May 3, 2013 }} Academy then-executive director Bruce Davis explained, "A Best Picture nomination should be an indication of extraordinary merit. If there are only eight pictures that truly earn that honor in a given year, we shouldn't feel an obligation to round out the number."{{cite news|url=http://www.ifc.com/fix/2011/06/new-academy-award-rules |title=An Idiot's Guide To The New Academy Award Rules |access-date=April 19, 2013 |last=Singer |first=Matt |work=[[Independent Film Channel|IFC]] |date=June 15, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130510024016/http://www.ifc.com/fix/2011/06/new-academy-award-rules |archive-date=May 10, 2013 }}{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2011/06/16/movies/oscar-rules-for-best-picture-change-yet-again.html |title=New Rules to Set Field for Chasing Top Oscar |access-date=April 19, 2013 |last=Cieply |first=Michael |work=The New York Times |date=June 15, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121119213536/http://www.nytimes.com/2011/06/16/movies/oscar-rules-for-best-picture-change-yet-again.html |archive-date=November 19, 2012 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} Changes in the Best Animated Feature also were announced. In response to the growing number of animated features released per year, the academy stated in a press release that four to five films would now be nominated per year contingent on how many animated feature films were released in that year.{{cite news|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/dorothypomerantz/2011/06/15/five-films-to-compete-for-best-picture-or-maybe-eight/ |title=Five Films to Compete for Best Picture ... Or Maybe Eight |access-date=April 19, 2013 |first=Dorothy |last=Pomerantz |work=[[Forbes]] |date=June 15, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130620085733/http://www.forbes.com/sites/dorothypomerantz/2011/06/15/five-films-to-compete-for-best-picture-or-maybe-eight/ |archive-date=June 20, 2013 }} }} === Box office performance of nominated films === For the first time since 2008, only one of the nominees for Best Picture had grossed over $100 million before the nominations were announced (compared with three from the previous year).{{cite news|url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/story/2012-02-08/best-picture-nominees-box-office/53015782/1 |title=Blockbusters left out of the best-picture Oscar race |work=[[USA Today]] |first=Scott |last=Bowles |date=February 8, 2012 |access-date=April 30, 2012 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131005044151/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/story/2012-02-08/best-picture-nominees-box-office/53015782/1 |archive-date=October 5, 2013 }} The combined gross of the nine Best Picture nominees when the Oscars were announced was $518 million with an average gross of $57.7 million per film.{{cite web|url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2011&p=.htm |title=2011 Academy Awards Nominations and Winner for Best Picture |publisher=[[Box Office Mojo]] |access-date=May 15, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130510003621/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2011&p=.htm |archive-date=May 10, 2013 }} None of the nine Best Picture nominees was among the top ten releases in box office during the nominations. When the nominations were announced on January 24, 2012, ''The Help'' was the highest-grossing film among the Best Picture nominees with $169.6 million in domestic box office receipts. Among the remaining eight nominees, ''Moneyball'' was the second-highest-grossing film with $75.5 million; this was followed by ''War Horse'' ($72.3 million), ''Midnight in Paris'' ($56.4 million), ''Hugo'' ($55.9 million), ''The Descendants'' ($51.3 million), ''The Tree of Life'' ($13.3 million), ''The Artist'' ($12.1 million) and ''Extremely Loud & Incredibly Close'' ($10.7 million).{{cite news|url=http://latimesblogs.latimes.com/movies/2012/01/box-office-help-academy-awards-moneyball.html |work=Los Angeles Times |title=Oscars 2012: 'The Help' has biggest box office among nominees |last=Kaufman |first=Amy |access-date=January 25, 2012 |date=January 25, 2012 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120127074505/http://latimesblogs.latimes.com/movies/2012/01/box-office-help-academy-awards-moneyball.html |archive-date=January 27, 2012 }} Of the top 50 grossing movies of the year, 36 nominations went to 15 films on the list. Only ''The Help'' (13th), ''Bridesmaids'' (14th), ''Kung Fu Panda 2'' (15th), ''Puss in Boots'' (16th), ''Rango'' (22nd), ''The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo'' (28th), ''Moneyball'' (43rd), and ''War Horse'' (46th) were nominated for Best Picture, Best Animated Feature or any of the directing, acting or screenwriting awards.{{cite news|url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=allmovies&yr=2011&sort=rank&order=ASC&p=.htm |title=2011 Oscar nominations and wins by movie |work=Box Office Mojo |access-date=April 27, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120130092403/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=allmovies&yr=2011&sort=rank&order=ASC&p=.htm |archive-date=January 30, 2012 |url-status=live }} The other top 50 box office hits that earned nominations were ''Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows – Part 2'' (1st), ''Transformers: Dark of the Moon'' (2nd), ''Rise of the Planet of the Apes'' (11th), ''Rio'' (18th), ''The Muppets'' (34th), ''Real Steel'' (35th), and ''The Adventures of Tintin'' (47th). === Critical reviews === The show received a mixed reception from media publications. Some media outlets were more critical of the show. Television critic Lori Rackl of the ''[[Chicago Sun-Times]]'' criticized Crystal's performance saying that the emcee "left his ''A'' game at home Sunday. Crystal's mediocre monologue was consistent with a mediocre 84th installment of Hollywood's biggest awards ceremony.{{cite news|url=http://www.suntimes.com/entertainment/television/10900231-421/had-enoughof-billys-fluff-songs.html |work=Chicago Sun-Times |first=Lori |last=Rackl |title=Enough with Billy Crystal's fluff songs |date=February 27, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004220311/http://www.suntimes.com/entertainment/television/10900231-421/had-enoughof-billys-fluff-songs.html |archive-date=October 4, 2013 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} Columnist Tim Goodman of ''[[The Hollywood Reporter]]'' quipped that "Somewhere, against all odds, [[James Franco]] is buying drinks for everybody." He went on to say that the [[83rd Academy Awards|previous year's]] critically panned telecast was eclipsed by Crystal's dull antics and that the show itself was "poorly paced as any in recent memory."{{cite news|first=Tim |last=Goodman |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/bastard-machine/review-oscars-Billy-Crystal-boring-The-Artist-Hugo-295309 |work=The Hollywood Reporter |title=Review: Oscars Become Badly Paced Bore-fest |date=February 27, 2012 |access-date=April 23, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120521135208/http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/bastard-machine/review-oscars-Billy-Crystal-boring-The-Artist-Hugo-295309 |archive-date=May 21, 2012 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} [[Alessandra Stanley]] of ''[[The New York Times]]'' lamented, "The whole night looked like an [[AARP]] pep rally." She also noted that, "For a town that prides itself on tinsel and titillation, the night was pretty tame."{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/02/27/movies/awardsseason/at-the-oscars-even-the-jokes-had-wrinkles.html |title=Even the Jokes Have Wrinkles |date=February 27, 2012 |first=Alessandra |last=Stanley |work=The New York Times |access-date=April 25, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121213204608/http://www.nytimes.com/2012/02/27/movies/awardsseason/at-the-oscars-even-the-jokes-had-wrinkles.html |archive-date=December 13, 2012 }} Other media outlets received the broadcast more positively. [[Ken Tucker]] of ''[[Entertainment Weekly]]'' commented that despite the ceremony running over three hours and honoring films that had earned modest box office numbers, "it was a jolly good show." He also praised the cast and several sketches and segments from the show.{{cite magazine|url=http://watching-tv.ew.com/2012/02/27/academy-awards-2012-oscars-billy-crystal/ |title=Academy Awards show review: Oscars in good hands with Billy Crystal... and Christopher Guest, and Emma Stone, and Angelina Jolie's leg |date=February 27, 2012 |first=Ken |last=Tucker |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |access-date=April 19, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130503003521/http://watching-tv.ew.com/2012/02/27/academy-awards-2012-oscars-billy-crystal/ |archive-date=May 3, 2013 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} Film critic [[Roger Ebert]] lauded Crystal's performance saying "As probably the most popular Oscar emcee, he astonished the audience by topping himself." Of the show itself, Ebert added that it was "an unqualified improvement" over the previous year's ceremony.{{cite news|url=https://www.rogerebert.com/festivals-and-awards/the-artist-and-hugo-a-very-french-oscars |work=Chicago Sun-Times |title="The Artist" and "Hugo": A very French Oscars |first=Roger |last=Ebert |date=February 26, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602101757/http://www.rogerebert.com/festivals-and-awards/the-artist-and-hugo-a-very-french-oscars |archive-date=June 2, 2013 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} [[Associated Press]] critic [[Frazier Moore]] pointed out that Crystal's performance "was nothing new or unexpected in his act", but he extolled him for stewarding "a sleek and entertaining Oscarcast."{{cite news|url=http://jacksonville.com/entertainment/tv/2012-02-27/story/tv-review-billy-crystal-returns-host-winning-oscarcast |title=TV Review: Billy Crystal returns to host a winning Oscarcast |date=February 27, 2012 |first=Frazier |last=Moore |agency=[[Associated Press]] |work=[[Florida Times-Union]] |access-date=April 19, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131012083228/http://jacksonville.com/entertainment/tv/2012-02-27/story/tv-review-billy-crystal-returns-host-winning-oscarcast |archive-date=October 12, 2013 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} === Ratings and reception === The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 39.46 million people over its length, which was a 4% increase from the previous year's ceremony.{{cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/tv-column/post/early-stats-may-suggest-oscar-ratings-uptick/2012/02/27/gIQA7KlydR_blog.html |title=Oscars 2012 ratings: About 39 million |archive-date=February 28, 2012 }} An estimated 76.56 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards.{{cite news|last=Gorman |first=Bill |title="Academy Awards" Broadcast Draws its 14, 2012 |url-status=dead |df=mdy}} The show also earned higher [[Nielsen ratings]] compared to the previous ceremony with 23.91% of households watching over a 37.64 share. However the program scored a sightly lower 18-49 demo rating with an 11.67 rating over a 32.68 share among viewers in that demographic, essentially flat with last year's numbers.{{cite news|url=http://tvline.com/2012/02/27/ratings-84th-academy-awards-oscars/ |title=Ratings: Oscars Inch Up Versus Last Year, Celebrity Apprentice Hits All-Time Low |last=Motivich |first=Matt Webb
